mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of Copy-on-Write (COW) in operating systems?

options.text: To reduce memory usage and improve performance during process creation
To encrypt memory pages for security
To compress memory pages to save space
To increase CPU utilization during forking

mcqs.explanation: COW is designed to optimize memory usage and performance by deferring the copying of memory pages until they are actually modified.

mcqs.question: How does COW handle memory pages immediately after a fork() operation?

options.text: It copies all pages immediately to the child process
It marks all pages as read-only and shares them between parent and child
It deletes all pages from the parent process
It allocates new physical memory for all pages

mcqs.explanation: Initially, COW shares all memory pages between parent and child, marking them as read-only to detect write attempts.

mcqs.question: What triggers the actual copying of a memory page in COW?

options.text: read operation on the page
write operation on the page
timer expiration
system call from the parent process

mcqs.explanation: The actual copying occurs only when either process attempts to write to a shared page, triggering a page fault.

mcqs.question: Which system call benefits most from COW optimization?

options.text: read()
write()
fork()
exec()

mcqs.explanation: COW is primarily an optimization for the fork() system call, which creates new processes.

mcqs.question: What happens when a page fault occurs in COW due to a write attempt?

options.text: The process is terminated
The OS creates a private copy of the page for the writing process
The page is marked as executable
The page is swapped to disk

mcqs.explanation: The OS handles the page fault by creating a private copy of the page, marking it as writable for the process that triggered the fault.
